label: Gerund (gerundio)
description: The gerund, Italian's non-finite -ando / -endo form (parlando, prendendo, dormendo). Formed regularly from the stem: -are gives -ando, -ere and -ire both give -endo. A small irregular set restores the Latin stem (fare gives facendo, dire gives dicendo, bere gives bevendo), the same expansion that drives their imperfect. Its headline job is the progressive construction stare + gerundio (sto parlando, stavo parlando), which works in any tense and is the reason this tree is built before the tense-usage work. It also serves as an adverbial (camminando, while/by walking) and has a compound form (gerundio passato: avendo finito). The gerund and the present progressive are A2; the compound and adverbial uses are B1-B2.

label: Formation
description: Building the gerund and its constructions. The regular forms (-are gives -ando, -ere/-ire give -endo), the small stem-expansion irregular set (facendo, dicendo, bevendo, ponendo, traducendo), the compound gerundio passato (avendo / essendo + participle), and the progressive assembly (stare conjugated in any tense + the gerund). This branch is what the GerundioFormation dispatch authors; usage (the adverbial gerund) and discrimination (progressive vs simple) are separate stubs.

label: -are gerund
description: The -are class forms the gerund in -ando: parlare gives parlando, mangiare gives mangiando, lavorare gives lavorando. The gerund is invariable (no person or number endings), so this is purely stem + -ando.

label: -ere / -ire gerund
description: Both the -ere and the -ire classes form the gerund in -endo: prendere gives prendendo, scrivere gives scrivendo, dormire gives dormendo, capire gives capendo. The -isc- of the present never appears (capire gives capendo, not capiscendo), and -ire does NOT give -iendo. One ending covers both classes.

attributes.common_miss: carrying -isc- (capiscendo), writing -iendo (capiendo), or using -ando on an -ere/-ire verb (prendando)

label: Stem-expansion irregulars
description: A small set restores the longer Latin stem before -endo, exactly the expansion that drives their imperfect: fare gives facendo (fac-, cf. facevo), dire gives dicendo (dic-, cf. dicevo), bere gives bevendo (bev-, cf. bevevo), porre gives ponendo, tradurre gives traducendo. essere gives essendo and avere gives avendo (regular on their own stems). If a learner knows the imperfect stem, the gerund stem is the same.

attributes.canonical_example: facendo, dicendo, bevendo, ponendo, essendo, avendo
attributes.common_miss: building from the contracted infinitive (facendo written as farendo, bevendo as berendo)

label: Progressive
description: The progressive construction: stare conjugated in the appropriate tense plus the invariable gerund. Present progressive (sto parlando) is A2 and the most frequent; the past progressive (stavo leggendo) is B1; future and conditional progressives (starò / starei leggendo) are B1-B2. The skill is the assembly: conjugate stare for the person and tense, then append the gerund. The stare conjugation itself is owned by the relevant tense tree (present, imperfect, future, conditional); this leaf tests putting the two together.

attributes.canonical_example: sto parlando, stai leggendo, stavo dormendo, starò lavorando
attributes.common_miss: wrong stare form for the person (sto / stai confusion), or pairing with an infinitive instead of a gerund (sto parlare)

label: Gerundio passato
description: The compound gerund: avendo or essendo plus the past participle (avendo finito, having finished; essendo andato/a, having gone). Expresses an action completed before the main clause ('Avendo finito i compiti, è uscito'). Reuses the auxiliary-choice and participle-agreement machinery of the passato prossimo, so items cross-reference those buckets; with essendo the participle agrees with the subject. B1-B2, lighter coverage.

attributes.canonical_example: avendo finito, essendo arrivato, essendo partita
attributes.common_miss: wrong auxiliary, or participle not agreeing with essendo (essendo andato for a feminine subject who needs andata)

label: Usage
description: When the bare (non-progressive) gerund is the right choice and what it conveys: the adverbial gerund of manner, means, time, cause, or condition (camminando, by/while walking; sbagliando si impara, one learns by making mistakes), and the same-subject constraint (the gerund's implicit subject must match the main clause, so 'vedendo Maria, l'ho salutata' means I saw her, not she saw me). Two authored leaves: manner/means (B1) and time/cause/condition incl. pur-concession (B2). Wave-2 usage batch accepted 2026-07-19.

label: Adverbial gerund: manner / means
description: The bare (non-progressive) gerund used adverbially to say HOW or BY WHAT MEANS the main action happens: è entrato correndo (he came in running), sbagliando s'impara (one learns by making mistakes), si mantiene in forma nuotando (he keeps fit by swimming). English 'by ...-ing' for means maps here, NOT onto per + infinitive (which is purpose). Governed by the same-subject constraint: the gerund's implicit subject must be the main clause's subject. B1 core.

attributes.canonical_example: sbagliando s'impara, è entrato correndo, imparo guardando i film
attributes.common_miss: using per + infinitive for means (per parlare instead of parlando); violating the same-subject constraint

label: Adverbial gerund: time / cause / condition
description: The bare gerund heading a subordinate of time, cause, condition or (with pur/pure) concession: tornando a casa (while coming home), essendo tardi (since it was late), volendo (if you want), pur essendo stanco (although tired). When the gerund's action precedes the main one, the compound gerund is used instead (avendo finito, essendo andato), so items here cross-evidence verb_form.gerundio.formation.gerundio_passato. Governed by the same-subject constraint. B2.

attributes.canonical_example: tornando a casa ho incontrato Lia, essendo tardi siamo andati via, pur essendo stanco ha finito
attributes.common_miss: violating the same-subject constraint (using a gerund when the subjects differ); using the simple gerund where anteriority needs the compound (finendo for avendo finito)

label: Discrimination
description: STUB. Choosing the progressive (stare + gerund) over the plain present where both are possible. Italian uses the plain present far more than English uses the simple, so 'mangio' often covers 'I am eating'; the progressive 'sto mangiando' is reserved for action genuinely in progress right now. The stub leaf below is its structural home, mirroring the tense_choice tree's progressive_vs_simple. Authored by TenseChoice; these buckets exist so cross-tree citations resolve.

attributes.superseded_note: Phantom duplicate: this skill is authored and LIVE under tense_choice (tense_choice owns cross-tense discrimination). Registered before tense_choice was established as the owner; never reconciled. DO NOT DISPATCH - authoring here would build a live skill a second time under a second bucket id and split the learner's stats. Kept, not deleted, so the reason survives and nobody re-scaffolds it. Ruled by Smith 2026-07-17; see STUB_CENSUS.md.

label: Progressive vs simple present
description: DEPRECATED AS AUTHORING TARGET (2026-06-09). Canonical home: tense_choice.progressive_vs_simple. Kept as a cross-reference pointer so cross-tree prerequisites and citations still resolve; do not author items here. STUB. When to use stare + gerund (action in progress right now) versus the plain present (which already covers most habitual and ongoing meaning in Italian). Mirrors the tense_choice tree's progressive_vs_simple. Authored by TenseChoice; this leaf is its structural home.
